text: This study examines the concepts of popular sovereignty and local elections within the framework of the 1945 Constitution of the Republic of Indonesia. At the heart of the current legal debate is the interpretation of the phrase "democratically elected" in Article 18, paragraph (4). Two primary perspectives have emerged: the first advocates for the direct election of local leaders by the people, while the second suggests that elections conducted by regional legislatures (DPRD) fulfill this constitutional requirement. By analyzing the broader constitutional context regarding general elections, this paper argues that local elections (Pilkada) are distinct from the national electoral regime governing the DPR, DPD, President, and Vice President. Ultimately, the study posits that a representative election system managed by the DPRD is a constitutionally valid democratic mechanism. Such a model aligns with the principle of popular sovereignty as characterized by Pancasila—emphasizing deliberation and consensus—as originally envisioned by Indonesia's founding fathers.
